sn22.47:1.1: Near Sāvatthī. There the Blessed One said,
sn22.47:1.2: “Monks, whatever contemplatives or brahmans who assume in various ways when assuming a self, all assume the five clinging-aggregates, or a certain one of them.
sn22.47:1.4: There is the case where an uninstructed, run-of-the-mill person—who has no regard for noble ones, is not well-versed or disciplined in their Dhamma; who has no regard for people of integrity, is not well-versed or disciplined in their Dhamma—
sn22.47:1.5: assumes form to be the self, or the self as possessing form, or form as in the self, or the self as in form.
sn22.47:1.6: He assumes feeling to be the self, or the self as possessing feeling, or feeling as in the self, or the self as in feeling.
sn22.47:1.7: He assumes perception to be the self, or the self as possessing perception, or perception as in the self, or the self as in perception.
sn22.47:1.8: He assumes fabrications to be the self, or the self as possessing fabrications, or fabrications as in the self, or the self as in fabrications.
sn22.47:1.9: He assumes consciousness to be the self, or the self as possessing consciousness, or consciousness as in the self, or the self as in consciousness.
sn22.47:2.1: Thus, both this assumption & the understanding, ‘I am,’ occur to him.
sn22.47:2.2: And so it is with reference to the understanding ‘I am’ that there is the appearance of the five faculties—
sn22.47:2.3: eye, ear, nose, tongue, & body [the senses of vision, hearing, smell, taste, & touch].
sn22.47:2.4: Now, there is the intellect, there are ideas [mental qualities], there is the property of ignorance.
sn22.47:2.5: To an uninstructed run-of-the-mill person, touched by experience born of the contact of ignorance, there occur (the thoughts): ‘I am,’
sn22.47:2.13: ‘I shall be neither percipient nor non-percipient.’
sn22.47:3.1: The five faculties, monks, continue as they were.
sn22.47:3.2: And with regard to them the well-instructed disciple of the noble ones abandons ignorance and gives rise to clear knowing.
sn22.47:3.3: Owing to the fading of ignorance and the arising of clear knowing, (the thoughts)—‘I am,’
sn22.47:3.11: ‘I shall be neither percipient nor non-percipient’—do not occur to him.”
